dataset_info.title: Indian Election Procedure & Election Commission of India
dataset_info.description: Comprehensive details on India's election procedures, voting technologies, special voting provisions, and the Election Commission of India

records.established_under: Article 324 of the Constitution of India
responsibilities: Superintendence, direction, and control of all elections to Parliament
Ensuring elections are free and fair without bias
records.residuary_powers: Where enacted laws are silent or insufficient, the ECI has residuary powers under the Constitution to act as appropriate (upheld by Supreme Court of India)
records.text: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an autonomous permanent constitutional body established under Article 324 of the Constitution. It oversees all parliamentary, state legislature, presidential and vice presidential elections. The Supreme Court has upheld that ECI holds residuary powers when laws are silent. India's first Chief Election Commissioner was Sukumar Sen.

elections_conducted: Members of Parliament in Rajya Sabha (Upper House)
Members of Parliament in Lok Sabha (Lower House)
Members of State Legislative Assemblies (Lower/Unicameral House)
Members of State Legislative Councils (Upper House)
Members of Legislative Assembly of National Capital Territory of Delhi
Members of Legislative Assembly of Union Territory of Jammu and Kashmir
Members of Legislative Assembly of Union Territory of Puducherry
records.text: The ECI conducts elections for the President and Vice President of India, both houses of Parliament (Rajya Sabha and Lok Sabha), State Legislative Assemblies and Councils, and the legislative assemblies of Delhi, Jammu & Kashmir, and Puducherry.

records.removal_process: Same manner as a Judge of a High Court
records.text: State Election Commissions (SECs) manage elections for local governance bodies — Panchayats and Municipalities. Each SEC is headed by a State Election Commissioner appointed by the Governor for a 5-year term, removable only like a High Court judge. There are 31 SECs across India.

responsibilities: Enforcing the Model Code of Conduct
Smooth conduct of Parliament and State Legislature elections
records.text: The Chief Electoral Officer (CEO) is a senior IAS officer nominated by the ECI to oversee elections at the state level. Each state and UT has a CEO responsible for voter registration, electoral rolls, law compliance, the Model Code of Conduct, and smooth election conduct.

responsibilities: Supervises all elections in the district
Conducts local government elections under SEC direction

roles.assigned_to: Each Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assembly constituency
responsibilities: Conducting the election in the constituency
Registering eligible home voters 10 days before election
Conducting election at polling booth with a team of Polling Officers

records.text: India's election machinery is layered: District Collectors serve as DEOs, overseeing Returning Officers for each constituency, who are assisted by AROs. Electoral Registration Officers manage voter lists. Booth Level Officers handle field-level voter registration. Presiding Officers with Polling Officers conduct elections at individual booths.

types.eligibility: All adult citizens of India aged 18 and above
types.tenure: 5 years or until dissolved by President

types.seats: Varies by state (based on size and population)
types.eligibility: All adult citizens in the state
types.tenure: 5 years or until dissolved by Governor

types.frequency: One-third of members re-elected every 2 years
types.seats: Up to 250 (238 elected + up to 12 nominated by President)
types.eligibility: Elected by members of State Legislative Assemblies (indirect election)
Elected by elected members of Parliament and State Legislative Assemblies (indirect election)
Elected by members of both Houses of Parliament

types.trigger: When a seat becomes vacant before term ends

records.text: India holds multiple types of elections: Lok Sabha every 5 years (543 seats), State Assembly elections every 5 years, Rajya Sabha elections on a rolling basis (one-third every 2 years, 6-year tenure), Presidential and Vice Presidential elections every 5 years, and By-elections whenever seats fall vacant mid-term.

records.voting_eligibility: All adult Indian citizens aged 18 and above
records.constituency_rule: Each adult citizen can vote only in their own constituency
records.meeting_location: Lok Sabha Chambers, Sansad Bhavan, New Delhi
records.purpose: Creation of new laws, removal or improvement of existing laws affecting all citizens
records.dissolution: By the President on advice of the Council of Ministers
records.text: Members of Lok Sabha are elected directly by all adult Indians (18+) from their respective constituencies. MPs hold seats for 5 years or until dissolution. The house meets at Sansad Bhavan, New Delhi, to create and amend national laws.

records.election_method: Indirect — elected by State Legislative Assembly members
records.presidential_nominations: Up to 12 members nominated by the President for contributions to art, literature, science, and social services
records.re_election_cycle: One-third of members face re-election every 2 years
records.role: Second-level review body — reviews bills before they become acts
records.money_bill_rule: Money bills must originate in Lok Sabha. Rajya Sabha can only recommend changes within 14 days. If not returned in 14 days, the bill is deemed passed.
records.bill_process: Can approve, reject, or return bills to Lok Sabha with amendments
records.text: The Rajya Sabha is India's upper house, with members indirectly elected by state assemblies for 6-year terms. Up to 12 members can be nominated by the President. Chaired by the Vice President, it reviews bills from Lok Sabha. Money bills can only originate in Lok Sabha; Rajya Sabha may suggest changes within 14 days.

records.definition: Election held when an elected seat becomes vacant before the term ends
triggers: Resignation of sitting MP or MLA
Death of sitting MP or MLA
Failure to maintain minimum level of attendance
Candidate wins more than one seat and must vacate one
records.text: By-elections (bypolls) fill vacated seats in the Lok Sabha, State Assemblies, or Rajya Sabha before term end. Triggers include death, resignation, criminal conviction, attendance failure, irregularities, or multi-seat wins requiring one seat to be vacated.

records.voter_identification: Indelible ink applied to left index finger after voting
records.campaigning_deadline: Ends at 6:00 PM two days before polling day
records.candidate_nomination: Candidates file nomination papers with the Electoral Commission
banned_practices: Use of government resources for campaigning
Starting government projects during election period
records.text: Indian elections run from 7AM to 6PM. District Collectors oversee polling with government employees as poll officers. EVMs are used to cast votes. After voting, the left index finger is marked with indelible ink (since 1962). Campaigning is banned 48 hours before polling. Government projects, resource use for campaigns, and bribery are prohibited during the election period.

vvpat.verification_count: 5 randomly selected EVMs per assembly constituency
vvpat.function: Produces a paper slip showing party name, serial number, and candidate image to allow voters to cross-check their vote
vvpat.post_2019_result: ECI declared no mismatches between EVM and VVPAT in 2019 General Election

records.text: EVMs replaced ballot boxes in 1997 and became the only voting method in 2004. VVPAT was introduced in Nagaland on 14 August 2014, piloted in 8 constituencies in 2014, and made mandatory nationwide from 2019 after a Supreme Court order requiring verification of 5 random EVMs per assembly constituency. Post-2019, ECI confirmed zero EVM-VVPAT mismatches.

records.rule: Even if NOTA gets more votes than any candidate, the candidate with the highest individual votes is still declared the winner
records.text: NOTA was introduced after a Supreme Court judgment on 27 September 2013 following a petition by the Electoral Commission and PUCL in 2009. First used in 5 state elections in November 2013. Even if NOTA wins the most votes, the candidate with the highest vote count is still declared elected.

records.purpose: Prevent multiple voting by the same person
records.research_body: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR)
records.research_location: Chemical Division, National Physical Laboratory of India
records.storage: Amber-colored plastic or brown-colored glass bottles
records.text: India's indelible election ink was developed by CSIR in the 1950s by M.L. Goel at the National Physical Laboratory. It contains photo-sensitive silver nitrate and lasts 2 days to 1 month on the left index finger. Applied to every voter since 1962 to prevent duplicate voting.

records.introduced_for: Senior citizens and persons with disabilities
records.nature: Voluntary — cannot be reversed once chosen
records.registration_process: Register with designated Booth Level Officer (BLO)
records.registration_deadline: At least 10 days before election date
records.documentation: Entire process documented via photographs and videos
records.2024_update: In 2024 Lok Sabha elections, ECI extended the facility to those aged 85+ and PwD voters for the first time in Lok Sabha history
records.text: The ECI allows voters 80+ (85+ from 2024) and disabled persons to vote from home via postal ballot. They must register 10 days in advance with their BLO using Form 12-D. A 5-member team including a polling officer, micro observer, police officer and photographer visits their home. Once selected, this option cannot be reversed.

records.process: Ballot papers distributed to registered eligible voters; votes returned by post
records.counting_order: Postal votes are counted before EVM votes
eligible_categories: Spouses of armed forces and state police
Government of India employees officially posted abroad
Persons above the age of 80 years
records.service_voters_term: Service voters (armed forces, state police, spouses, government employees abroad)
records.absentee_voting_note: India does not provide general absentee voting. Non-resident Indians were granted voting rights in 2010 (Representation of the People Amendment Bill) but physical presence at polling booth is still required.
records.text: India's postal voting uses the ETPB system. Eligible voters include military, police, their spouses, government employees abroad, people in preventive detention, disabled persons, and those above 80. Prisoners cannot vote. Postal votes are counted before EVM votes. General absentee voting is not available; NRIs must be physically present to vote despite 2010 law changes.

records.definition_of_bill: A draft of a legislative proposal brought before either house of Parliament
passing_process: Bill introduced in either Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha
Passed by both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha
Assented to by the President of India
records.money_bill_rule: Money bills must originate in Lok Sabha only. Rajya Sabha can only recommend changes within 14 days.
records.rajya_sabha_role: Can approve, reject, or return bills with suggested amendments
records.lok_sabha_supremacy: If Lok Sabha rejects all Rajya Sabha amendments, the bill is deemed passed by both Houses in its Lok Sabha form
records.text: A bill is a draft legislative proposal. It becomes an Act of Parliament only after being passed by both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha and receiving Presidential assent. Money bills must originate in Lok Sabha. If Lok Sabha rejects Rajya Sabha's amendments, the Lok Sabha version is deemed passed by both houses.
